nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy areas, seagrass beds, and rocky substrates in sheltered coastal waters
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; anchors via rhizoids into sand or rubble; tolerates low-light and turbid conditions typical of seagrass meadows

avoidWith: tanks with no light schedule control

imageCandidates.notes: Krzysztof Ziarnek (Kenraiz), 4608x3072. Species-labelled display shot; sharp, vibrant green, clearly shows the flat strap-like seagrass-like fronds diagnostic of C. prolifera.
Coughdrop12, 4032x3024. Very sharp vibrant close-up showing the creeping stolon and rhizoids anchoring the strap fronds; strong growth-form detail (title is genus-level).
Roberto Pillon, 700x525. In-situ wild specimen rooted in clean sand, bright green flat blades; adds a natural-habitat angle but lower resolution.

summary: A green macroalgae with long, flat, strap-like fronds native to Atlantic and Mediterranean coastal waters. Commonly kept in refugiums to export nitrate and phosphate from the system. Fast-growing and beginner-friendly, though it releases compounds that can stress nearby corals.
careNotes:
Caulerpa prolifera is a siphonous green macroalgae — meaning the entire plant is a single continuous cell rather than individual cells joined together — with long, flat, strap-like fronds resembling seagrass blades. These fronds grow from a creeping stolon (a horizontal runner) that anchors into sand or rubble via root-like structures called rhizoids. It is kept both as a refugium nutrient-export species and as a display tank specimen.

ALLELOPATHY: Caulerpa prolifera is documented to release secondary metabolites — specifically caulerpin and caulerpenyne — into the water column. These compounds have been shown to inhibit the growth of or stress nearby corals and other algae. Placing it in direct proximity to sensitive SPS corals (stony corals with small polyps) is generally not advised. Physical separation between the algae and corals, or running activated carbon in the system, is commonly recommended to manage this.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: Under stress conditions — particularly sharp drops in nutrients, a disrupted photoperiod, or rapid temperature changes — Caulerpa species including C. prolifera can undergo a process called holocarpy, in which the entire organism converts its internal contents to reproductive cells and effectively dies. This event releases large quantities of organic matter and gametes into the water column, which can cause a severe, acute water-quality crash (ammonia spike, oxygen depletion) that may devastate a reef tank. Reducing this risk involves several practices: running refugium lighting on a 24-hour cycle with no dark period, which is thought to suppress reproductive triggering; harvesting the algae regularly to prevent overgrowth and keep the colony in active growth phase; maintaining stable nutrient levels rather than allowing them to drop to zero; and removing immediately any specimen showing early signs of reproductive activity (fronds turning white or translucent, or a sudden turbid release into the water).

INVASIVE CONCERN: Caulerpa prolifera and close relatives — notably C. taxifolia and C. racemosa — have become serious marine invasive species in the Mediterranean. Captive specimens must NEVER be released into natural waterways. Sale is prohibited in some US states, including California and Hawaii, due to invasive risk.

GENERAL CARE: The species grows rapidly under medium-to-high reef lighting (5,000K color temperature or higher, with a photoperiod of 8 or more hours, or a 24-hour cycle in a refugium setting). It tolerates moderate water flow; gentle tumbling works well in a refugium chamber. Supplementing with iron, magnesium, and trace elements is generally considered beneficial, though it is not strictly required in an otherwise healthy reef system.

UPON ARRIVAL: Fronds may appear white or translucent after shipping due to chloroplast displacement during darkness in transit. This is a normal stress response and typically reverses within hours of exposure to light.
breedingNotes: Propagates readily via fragmentation — pieces as small as a few millimetres have been reported to grow into new plants. Also spreads by vegetative tip growth along the stolon.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros
additional.notes: Secondary trade source. Product page has minimal care data; confirms growth form (seagrass-like blades), refugium/display suitability, prolific growth, and invasive designation. Corroborates AlgaeBarn primary.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa prolifera
additional.notes: Taxonomic cross-check: Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales, Class Ulvophyceae confirmed. Native range (Atlantic, Mediterranean) confirmed. Holocarpy (going sexual) behavior documented, including Hurricane Wilma 2005 mass spawning event triggered by rapid temperature drop. Fragmentation reproduction confirmed.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
